subject: ap-precalculus
title: Polynomial Modeling: Covariation, Finite Differences & Concentration Analysis
stimulus:
After a patient takes a medication, the concentration of the drug in the bloodstream (in micrograms per milliliter) can be modeled by a polynomial function $C$ for $0 \leq t \leq 9$, where $t$ is time in hours after taking the medication. Selected values of $C(t)$ are given in the table below.

| $t$ (hours) | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$ | $5$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C(t)$ ($\mu$g/mL) | $0.5$ | $3.5$ | $12.5$ | $25.1$ | $38.9$ | $51.5$ |

parts.prompt:
(i) Compute the average rates of change of $C$ over the intervals $[0, 1]$, $[1, 2]$, $[2, 3]$, and $[3, 4]$. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) Based on the first differences (average rates of change over unit intervals) computed in part (i), determine whether the function $C$ is better modeled by a linear function or a higher-degree polynomial on the interval $0 \leq t \leq 4$. Justify your answer by referring to what the first differences would look like if $C$ were linear.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), all four differences are over unit-length intervals, so average rate equals the simple difference. The values $C(0)=0.5$, $C(1)=3.5$, $C(2)=12.5$, $C(3)=25.1$, $C(4)=38.9$ give differences $3.0, 9.0, 12.6, 13.8$. For part (ii), if the function were linear, all first differences would be equal (constant). The first differences here are $3.0, 9.0, 12.6, 13.8$ â€” clearly not constant, so the model is not linear. A higher-degree polynomial (or non-linear model) is appropriate. A response that computes the differences correctly but does not explain what constant first differences indicate does not earn the second point.

scoring_points.description: Correct conclusion with justification â€” the first differences $3.0, 9.0, 12.6, 13.8$ are not constant, so $C$ is not linear (a linear function would have constant first differences); the varying first differences indicate a higher-degree polynomial; the justification must explicitly state that linear functions have constant first differences and compare this to the data

required_elements: Correct conclusion with justification â€” the first differences $3.0, 9.0, 12.6, 13.8$ are not constant, so $C$ is not linear (a linear function would have constant first differences); the varying first differences indicate a higher-degree polynomial; the justification must explicitly state that linear functions have constant first differences and compare this to the data
alternatives.correct_example: Correct conclusion with justification â€” the first differences $3.0, 9.0, 12.6, 13.8$ are not constant, so $C$ is not linear (a linear function would have constant first differences); the varying first differences indicate a higher-degree polynomial; the justification must explicitly state that linear functions have constant first differences and compare this to the data

parts.prompt:
(i) Compute the second differences of $C$ (the differences of consecutive first differences from part (a)). Based on the second differences, determine whether a quadratic polynomial is an appropriate model for $C$ on $0 \leq t \leq 4$. Justify your answer.

(ii) Find the average rate of change of $C$ from $t = 1$ to $t = 5$ hours. Express your answer with appropriate units, and describe in context what this value represents about the drug concentration.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), the three second differences are $6.0, 3.6, 1.2$. These are not constant, ruling out a quadratic model. A quadratic would have constant second differences. For part (ii), $C(1) = 3.5$ and $C(5) = 51.5$ are given in the table. The exact average rate is $48.0/4 = 12.0$ $\mu$g/mL/hr. The contextual description requires all three components: direction (increasing), rate (12.0), and time interval (1 to 5 hours). Units are required for full credit.

scoring_points.description: Correct second differences and conclusion â€” second differences: $9.0 - 3.0 = 6.0$; $12.6 - 9.0 = 3.6$; $13.8 - 12.6 = 1.2$; the second differences $6.0, 3.6, 1.2$ are not constant; conclusion: a quadratic model is not appropriate because a quadratic function would produce constant second differences; the data suggest a cubic or higher-degree polynomial

required_elements: Correct second differences and conclusion â€” second differences: $9.0 - 3.0 = 6.0$; $12.6 - 9.0 = 3.6$; $13.8 - 12.6 = 1.2$; the second differences $6.0, 3.6, 1.2$ are not constant; conclusion: a quadratic model is not appropriate because a quadratic function would produce constant second differences; the data suggest a cubic or higher-degree polynomial
alternatives.correct_example: Correct second differences and conclusion â€” second differences: $9.0 - 3.0 = 6.0$; $12.6 - 9.0 = 3.6$; $13.8 - 12.6 = 1.2$; the second differences $6.0, 3.6, 1.2$ are not constant; conclusion: a quadratic model is not appropriate because a quadratic function would produce constant second differences; the data suggest a cubic or higher-degree polynomial

scoring_points.description: Correct average rate of change â€” $\frac{C(5) - C(1)}{5 - 1} = \frac{51.5 - 3.5}{4} = \frac{48.0}{4} = 12.0$ micrograms per milliliter per hour; contextual description must state (a) the concentration is increasing on average, (b) at a rate of $12.0$ $\mu$g/mL per hour, and (c) over the time interval from $1$ to $5$ hours after taking the medication; all three components required

required_elements: Correct average rate of change â€” $\frac{C(5) - C(1)}{5 - 1} = \frac{51.5 - 3.5}{4} = \frac{48.0}{4} = 12.0$ micrograms per milliliter per hour; contextual description must state (a) the concentration is increasing on average, (b) at a rate of $12.0$ $\mu$g/mL per hour, and (c) over the time interval from $1$ to $5$ hours after taking the medication; all three components required
alternatives.correct_example: Correct average rate of change â€” $\frac{C(5) - C(1)}{5 - 1} = \frac{51.5 - 3.5}{4} = \frac{48.0}{4} = 12.0$ micrograms per milliliter per hour; contextual description must state (a) the concentration is increasing on average, (b) at a rate of $12.0$ $\mu$g/mL per hour, and (c) over the time interval from $1$ to $5$ hours after taking the medication; all three components required

parts.prompt:
A doctor determines the medication is effective only when the concentration exceeds $20$ micrograms per milliliter. The complete model is given by $C(t) = -0.4t^3 + 4.2t^2 - 0.8t + 0.5$ for $0 \leq t \leq 9$.

(i) Use technology to find all times $t$ in $0 \leq t \leq 9$ at which $C(t) = 20$. Express the values as decimal approximations to the nearest hundredth.

(ii) State the time interval(s) during which the medication is effective. Then find the value of $C(9)$ and interpret its meaning in the context of this problem.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), $C(9) = -0.4(729) + 4.2(81) - 0.8(9) + 0.5 = -291.6 + 340.2 - 7.2 + 0.5 = 41.9 > 20$. There is only one crossing in $[0, 9]$ near $t \approx 2.62$. For part (ii), the effective interval is approximately $[2.62, 9]$. $C(9) \approx 41.9$ means the medication remains effective at the end of the modeled period. The contextual interpretation must reference the $20$ $\mu$g/mL threshold.

scoring_points.description: Correct times â€” solving $-0.4t^3 + 4.2t^2 - 0.8t + 0.5 = 20$, i.e., $-0.4t^3 + 4.2t^2 - 0.8t - 19.5 = 0$; using technology, the relevant root in $[0, 9]$ is $t \approx 2.62$ (accept values in $[2.58, 2.66]$); $C(9) \approx 41.9 > 20$ so the concentration stays above 20 through $t = 9$

required_elements: Correct times â€” solving $-0.4t^3 + 4.2t^2 - 0.8t + 0.5 = 20$, i.e., $-0.4t^3 + 4.2t^2 - 0.8t - 19.5 = 0$; using technology, the relevant root in $[0, 9]$ is $t \approx 2.62$ (accept values in $[2.58, 2.66]$); $C(9) \approx 41.9 > 20$ so the concentration stays above 20 through $t = 9$
alternatives.correct_example: Correct times â€” solving $-0.4t^3 + 4.2t^2 - 0.8t + 0.5 = 20$, i.e., $-0.4t^3 + 4.2t^2 - 0.8t - 19.5 = 0$; using technology, the relevant root in $[0, 9]$ is $t \approx 2.62$ (accept values in $[2.58, 2.66]$); $C(9) \approx 41.9 > 20$ so the concentration stays above 20 through $t = 9$

scoring_points.description: Correct effective interval stated as approximately $[2.62, 9]$ (or equivalent using the student's $t$-value from part (i)); AND correct value $C(9) \approx 41.9$ micrograms per milliliter with interpretation that the concentration is still above the effective threshold at the end of the modeled period

required_elements: Correct effective interval stated as approximately $[2.62, 9]$ (or equivalent using the student's $t$-value from part (i)); AND correct value $C(9) \approx 41.9$ micrograms per milliliter with interpretation that the concentration is still above the effective threshold at the end of the modeled period
alternatives.correct_example: Correct effective interval stated as approximately $[2.62, 9]$ (or equivalent using the student's $t$-value from part (i)); AND correct value $C(9) \approx 41.9$ micrograms per milliliter with interpretation that the concentration is still above the effective threshold at the end of the modeled period
